title: Google's New AI Architecture Changes Everything (Gemma 4 12B)
summary: Google DeepMind released Gemma 4 12B, a multimodal LLM that processes images and audio by reading raw pixels and waveforms directly instead of using separate encoder models. The architecture eliminates intermediate encoder models, enabling faster offline performance on consumer hardware like Apple Silicon.
writeup: Google DeepMind's Gemma 4 12B model eliminates the separate vision and audio encoders that every other multimodal model relies on, replacing them with a single thin linear projection layer of only 35 million parameters. Where traditional multimodal models tape together multiple networks (a vision encoder alone can run 550 million parameters) and saturate VRAM before the language backbone even receives data, Gemma 4 maps raw 48x48 pixel patches and 40-millisecond audio frames directly into the LLM's token space in one static mathematical step. The main transformer backbone handles all visual and audio reasoning natively, shrinking the total footprint enough to run on a standard 16 GB VRAM laptop while benchmarking close to Google's own 26B parameter models.
key_points: Gemma 4's encoder-free architecture replaces a conventional 550-million-parameter vision encoder with a single 35-million-parameter linear projection layer that reformats raw 48x48 pixel patches (2,304 color values each) into the LLM's hidden-dimension token format without performing any internal visual analysis.

Audio input is handled by slicing a 16 kHz signal into 40-millisecond frames of 640 floating-point values each, then projecting those floats directly into the transformer's input space; because audio is already a chronological sequence like text, the backbone treats it as a continuous token stream, enabling live transcription and translation in a single forward pass.

By offloading all visual and audio reasoning to the main language backbone rather than dedicated encoder networks, Gemma 4 12B fits in 16 GB of VRAM, eliminating the multi-network VRAM saturation that makes traditional multimodal models impractical on consumer hardware.

Google included native multi-token prediction drafters in the released model, allowing faster local inference speeds without quantization-only tradeoffs; the presenter tested an 8-bit quantized version via MLX on an M2 MacBook Pro with 24 GB of unified memory and observed real-time image parsing.

On image-reasoning benchmarks Gemma 4 12B approaches the performance of Google's own 26B parameter models, demonstrating that a sufficiently deep language backbone can substitute for a dedicated vision encoder without a proportional accuracy cost.

The linear projection is a static, single-layer weight matrix: it performs zero analytical thinking and is identical for every image, which is why it adds almost no processing overhead and why the 35-million-parameter count reflects only the raw connection weights needed for dimensional reformatting.
